featured.title: Spreewald Day Trip from Berlin: Punts, Pickles and the Right Train (2026)
featured.summary: The Spreewald is Berlin's best nature escape: a UNESCO biosphere reserve of roughly 300 canals, about an hour away by train. Here is the station most guides get wrong, what punts and kayaks cost in 2026, and how to shape the day.
featured.alt: Punts full of visitors setting off across the harbor basin in Lübbenau
featured.label: Newest practical day trip

miniPosts.title: Stolpersteine in Berlin: How to Read the Brass Stones Under Your Feet (2026)
miniPosts.summary: Berlin’s pavements hold more than 11,000 small brass memorials, and most visitors walk over them without seeing one. The text on every stone follows the same compact formula. Learn it once, in about ten minutes, and the city never looks the same.
miniPosts.alt: Four brass Stolpersteine for the Salinger family set into dark cobblestones in a Berlin pavement

miniPosts.title: Is Berlin Walkable? How Far Apart the Main Sights Really Are (2026)
miniPosts.summary: Is Berlin walkable? The central sights are far closer than the map suggests, and flat underfoot. Here is how far apart they really are, the one walk to do on foot, and when to take the train instead.
miniPosts.alt: The Bode Museum dome and the TV Tower rising across the Spree on Berlin's Museum Island, two of the central sights that sit within an easy walk of each other

miniPosts.title: Berliner Unterwelten: How to Actually Get Into Berlin's Bunker Tours (2026)
miniPosts.summary: Berlin kept most of its twentieth century underground, and the only way in is a guided tour you have to book properly. Here is which of the tours to pick, why they do not all start at Gesundbrunnen, and how the 30 day ticket window works.
miniPosts.alt: The concrete ruin of the Humboldthain flak tower in Volkspark Humboldthain, Berlin

miniPosts.title: Berlin U-Bahn Etiquette: The Unwritten Rules Tourists Break (2026)
miniPosts.summary: Berlin U-Bahn etiquette mixes formal VBB rules with local habits. Here is what to do at doors, escalators, seats and ticket checks, plus current food and bike rules.
miniPosts.alt: A yellow Berlin U-Bahn train stopped at a platform with its doors open, showing the interior seats.

posts.title: Where to Watch the 2026 World Cup in Berlin (No, There's No Brandenburg Gate Fan Mile This Year)
posts.summary: The 2026 World Cup is in North America, and Berlin has no Brandenburg Gate fan mile this year. Here is where to actually watch: free screens, beer gardens and bars, with every kick-off in Berlin time.
posts.alt: A summer evening football public viewing in Berlin with a relaxed crowd, outdoor screen and TV Tower skyline

posts.title: Schöneberg Berlin: Bowie, Kennedy and the District That Whispers (2026)
posts.summary: Schöneberg keeps its history at eye level, on plaques screwed to buildings people still live in. Here is what is actually worth the U-Bahn ride: the Saturday market, the Kennedy square, the Bayerisches Viertel memorial, and the honest truth about the Bowie flat.
posts.alt: A cheese and egg stall at Winterfeldtmarkt with a stallholder serving customers under white awnings, the tower of the Apostel-Paulus-Kirche behind

posts.title: How Many Days Do You Need in Berlin? An Honest Local's Answer (2026)
posts.summary: Berlin is big on the map but small where it counts. Here is how many days you actually need, what each extra day unlocks, and when a day trip is worth it.
posts.alt: Warm high-angle view over central Berlin with the TV Tower rising above Mitte and the River Spree
